I will disagree with the takes on education and art as far as AI goes. What I am seeing is that both the ability to prompt AI and to curate AI responses are essential differentiators between users. In other words, the more you yourself know about the subject, the better you are able to use AI to assist you. If you always lean on AI to cut corners and cheat your way through school, you don't learn these kinds of skills. Most students apparently lack the maturity or insight to understand this. So, banning AI in most classroom activities is essential for encouraging actual learning, since students obviously can't resist the temptation to have it cheat for them. In a competitive job market, it will be the people who can use AI effectively who rise above the others, and ironically that requires being able to stand on their own two legs without AI helping you. As an example, I recently read of a brilliant mathematician who is having high level discussions with AI to advance his theories. Those discussions are only possible because of what he already knows, making him expert enough to ask insightful questions. The use of AI in the classroom discourages the development of that kind of expertise. As for AI making art, AI will be able to make art as good as humans the day that AI is able to be your best friend. Until it has that level of empathy and emotional ability, it will always fall short. And the fact that AI will collapse if it is only trained on AI gives a solid clue that whatever makes it tick, it's not any internal emotional selfhood that is bringing genuine emotional responses and the attendant artistic insight and imagination that a human has. Humans would never collapse mentally if they were only given AI art to learn from; their emotions would inform them, leading to self expression in new creative directions. AI is devoid of this ability. It will never make any art that is truly great. It can make some formulaic art, but that's it.
AI is growing faster than human capacity, but that is not the main problem. The real issue is leaving security behind and not developing both together at the same time.

I am doing my thesis on this area to provide an approach(hopefully with a prototype) for big guys(c suite executives) to first know the Regulations and their responsibilities, then through a dashboard they can see the flow of process based on standards(ISO 27000 family, NIST, CIS)  through a regular auditable, iterative, context based RMP(Risk Management Program). So in this way they consciously assign authorities and each decision would be accooring to identified/understood Risks not just Expert's opinions. My goal is to emphsis the governance and decision making stage.

After signing in with a passkey or a physical security key for multi-factor authentication, the browser stores a session cookie so you don't have to tap your key for every new page you visit. Can a hacker steal that session cookie and access your account on another device without your passkey or security key, and can DBSC (Device-Bound Session Credentials) prevent this?

This is called session hijacking and, yes, it can happen but the web site can also prevent it. Having a TLS encrypted session helps if the web site detects that the cookie is being used over a different encrypted path
